conclusion: Nike is the perfect Acquired case study for product vs marketing — answer: neither alone. A fabless ~$51B apparel giant (10% YoY growth) that manufactures zero shoes but dominates a ~$130B US athletic-footwear market it helped grow from ~$2B. Phil Knight's Blue Ribbon imported Tigers; Rob Strasser invented modern sports marketing; Michael Jordan saved the company and changed culture. Jordan Brand now ~$6B+ revenue growing ~35% — Jordan may earn $5–10B lifetime. Ben and David argue Nike's Seven Powers center on scale economies funding athlete 'Netflix shows' and $4B 'demand creation,' not classic brand pricing power (Dunks priced like Adidas). Strategic shift underway: digital/direct, 12-city marketing, NFL/NBA/MLB uniform deals paying for swooshes not jersey revenue. Air bags made in Oregon; Asia assembly. Trap: athletes hold wholesale transfer pricing power; Nike chooses volume of swooshes over $500 SKUs.
background: Season 13 opener with Scott Reames (Nike historian) and ex-COO Eric Sprunk input. Origin: Bill Bowerman Oregon track coach; Phil Knight 1962 Stanford GSB Japan trip; Blue Ribbon 51/49 with Bowerman; $8K revenue 1964 → $150M 1979. Onitsuka split; Carolyn Davidson swoosh ($35, 500 IPO shares worth ~$7M today). Strasser (ex-lawyer) built college basketball (Georgetown, UNLV) and Jordan deal — Air movie credits Vaccaro over Strasser inaccurately. Jordan year-one $126M included channel stuffing; Tinker Hatfield/Jumpman saved Jordan from Adidas; 5% royalty, Jordan sub-brand. Just Do It 1988; market cap ~$400M at 1980 IPO (~$22M raised), Apple $1.8B then. Today: ~$51B revenue; women alone ~$8.6B (> Lululemon); $4B demand creation; ~$8.5B inventory; ~$10.7B cash. Power analysis: scale economies > brand pricing; no switching costs. Fabless like TSMC/Nvidia but shoe factories commoditized.
important_facts: Nike ~$51B revenue growing ~10% YoY — largest apparel company ex-luxury; US branded athletic shoe market ~$130B today vs ~$2B when Knight started (5% YoY still); technically manufactures zero shoes (fabless model).

Michael Jordan first-year Air Jordan ~$126M revenue (~15% of Nike that year) with channel stuffing; 5% royalty earned ~$6.3M year one matching his entire 7-year Bulls contract; Jordan Brand now ~$6B+ growing ~35% — Jordan lifetime Nike earnings may reach $5–10B.

Rob Strasser architected sports marketing (college basketball table-run late 1970s) and Jordan deal; left for Adidas America (Portland HQ persists), died age 46; Nike 1977 principles ('on offense all the time') typed by Strasser.
1980 IPO ~$400M market cap, ~$22M raised; Phil Knight ~46% ownership worth ~$178M then (~$40B today) — built on debt not equity; Bowerman sold back stake pre-IPO.

Demand creation ~$4B/year; official NFL/NBA/MLB uniform supplier — NFL ~$200M/year without selling replica jerseys (billboard strategy); Nike ID/By You personalization at scale; Air nitrogen bags 100% manufactured Oregon then shipped to Asia.

mental_model.name: Scale-Funded Athlete Halo
mental_model.components: Nike sells dream via athletes as 'Netflix shows' (Strasser 1983 memo: heroes of what real people can't do). $4B demand creation buys media like Netflix content — monetized via footwear/apparel volume not subscription. Scale economies: only Nike can afford LeBron/Jordan-tier deals plus $200M NFL swoosh; competitors stuck in middle (Under Armour mini-Nike trap). Brand power manifests as consideration ('I only look at Nike') not price premium vs Adidas/New Balance at $160–180. Secondary market value (Goat/StockX) intentionally uncaptured — trades swooshes for ubiquity. Fabless manufacturing: commoditized assembly in audited Asian factories; differentiated Air bags in Oregon. Jordan counter-positioned 1984 — Magic/Bird wouldn't accept unequal deals. Wholesale transfer pricing: pinnacle athletes extract surplus.
mental_model.application: Consumer brands should separate halo spend from unit economics — Nike accepts lower gross margin vs Adidas for swoosh density. Don't copy Nike playbook without scale (Brooks or Nike, not Under Armour). Asset-light works when R&D/secrets are narrow (Air) not whole shoe. Leadership transitions (John Donahoe post-Parker) and sport-silo reorg are bear cases; scale + basketball/sneakerhead core remain bull.

competitive_advantage: Nike's moat is scale economies applied to athlete sponsorship and global demand creation — ~2× Adidas, Adidas ~3× Skechers (#3), power-law footwear concentration unique in apparel. Strasser playbook: college coaches (Jimmy V, Georgetown), then Jordan sub-brand autonomy (hidden swoosh, Jumpman) — competitors couldn't counter-position without angering existing stars. Just Do It + ESPN/SportsCenter timing amplified halo. Manufacturing: fabless with prescriptive supplier control; dominant factory capacity allocation favors largest orders. Air/Sole trade secrets kept US. Futures Program stabilized revenue through downturns. Digital: Nike app ecosystem (Run Club, SNKRS), DTC migration from 2014, personalization at scale. League uniform deals buy game-day swooshes without jersey COGS. Weaknesses: no switching costs; athlete power captures margin; MeToo/Oregon Project scandals; inventory ~$8.5B elevated; women underdeveloped vs ~$8.6B revenue; Kanye/Adidas cautionary tale — Nike stayed athlete-focused. Commodity Monarch volume vs Vaporfly R&D prestige tier. vs luxury: won't sell $500+ regular SKUs — volume not markup; secondary hype benefits resellers not Nike P&L. Post-Jordan, Nike institutionalized athlete-as-media: college basketball coaches (Jimmy V, Georgetown) wore swooshes on national TV even when fans could not buy cleats — the funnel is impression volume, not SKU match. Futures Program locked retailer commitments six months ahead, smoothing 1980s Reebok crisis. Global outsourcing followed yen float post-Nixon; Nissho Iwai 4% royalty on all sales funded inventory when Oregon banks refused growth. Today women’s ~$8.6B exceeds Lululemon; DTC and 12-city athlete storytelling replace broad TV. Weakness: wholesale athletes extract margin — Jordan lifetime economics may reach $5–10B, dwarfing Nike’s share of Jordan Brand ~$6B+ revenue. Air Sole (Frank Rudy, NASA engineer): Phil initially rejected idea until hearing Adidas passed — Bowerman-era Oregon mad-science culture. Pre-IPO convertible notes from Woodell family and employees. Demand creation ~$4B today buys athlete stories as content, not CPM-optimized ads. Seven Powers episode conclusion: scale economies > brand pricing power; Dunks priced like Adidas despite hype. Vietnam/China labor controversy 1990s was predictable endpoint of 1962 Stanford arbitrage thesis — public expected ‘inspiring greatness,’ not supplier finger-pointing.

key_insights.view: Nike is marketing AND product — via scale.
key_insights.question: Product or marketing company?
key_insights.answer: Ben/David reject binary: Strasser invented sports marketing; Bowerman/R&D (Air, Vaporfly) innovates product — but durable profit is scale-funded halo. $4B demand creation + athlete deals require ~$51B revenue base competitors lack. Prestige Jordans/Vaporfly are products; $60 Monarchs are brand-halo commodities.

key_insights.question: Does Nike have Hamilton brand power?
key_insights.answer: Trap episode: swoosh drives consideration but running shoes price-match Adidas/New Balance at $160–180 — Nike may choose growth over harvesting brand via price. Power shows in tattoos, secondary markets, and 'can't kill Nike in 100 years' durability debate — not Tiffany-style markup.

key_insights.view: Jordan saved more than the company.
key_insights.question: What did Jordan change?
key_insights.answer: 1984 deal with 5% royalty and sub-brand (Tinker/Jumpman after Strasser-Adidas scare) created template for athlete-as-founder ($6B+ Jordan Brand, ~35% growth). Year-one $126M included channel stuffing but cultural shift permanent. Air movie wrong: Strasser's baby, not Vaccaro alone.

key_insights.view: Fabless shoe company is the answer.
key_insights.question: Does Nike make shoes?
key_insights.answer: Zero shoes assembled by Nike; ~100% of nitrogen Air bags made Oregon — analogous to fabless semis (TSMC/Nvidia) except shoe assembly is commoditized. Thousands of apparel factories vs few audited footwear partners — scale wins factory capacity.

key_insights.question: Why pay NFL ~$200M/year?
key_insights.answer: Nike replaced Adidas on NBA jerseys etc. but often can't sell official game jerseys — pays for swoosh on broadcast billboards and replica halo, not apparel COGS recovery. Official supplier status reinforces scale-economy marketing machine tied to 12 global cities strategy.

top_investment_implications.thesis: ~$51B revenue, ~10% growth, ~35% Jordan Brand growth, and scale-economy demand creation (~$4B) support durable lead vs Adidas — but apparel margins not software; athlete power, inventory ~$8.5B, and CEO transition cap multiple expansion; bull case is global swoosh ubiquity and basketball/sneakerhead strength.

Adidas Tegus interviews cite 'make brand cool again' — structural scale disadvantage vs Nike's athlete-halo spend; Portland Adidas America HQ legacy of Strasser poaching shows long competitive shadow but #2 position lacks Nike's uniform deals and Jordan-tier sub-brand.

golden_quotes: "Individual athletes, even more than teams, will be the heroes. Symbols more and more of what real people can't do anymore, risk and win" — Rob Strasser 1983 internal memo, predicting athlete-as-hero marketing Nike still runs.
"Nike Inc is a growth company" — Ben citing Nike IR positioning explaining willingness to trade margin for swoosh ubiquity vs raising prices above Adidas parity.
"Michael Jordan will make five, maybe $10 billion over his lifetime from the Jordan brand" — Ben on 2023 Jordan Brand scale (~$6B+ revenue, ~35% growth) dwarfing all active athlete earnings.
